vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Metabolism of Vinyl Chloride (VC; CAS 75-01-4) in Humans

1. Entry and overall disposition  
   • Main exposure route: inhalation (→ rapid pulmonary absorption; >40 % retained per breath at rest).  
   • First-pass organ: liver (≈ 90 % of systemically available VC is cleared in a single passage through the hepatic circulation).  
   • VC is highly volatile, poorly water-soluble, and not ionised at physiological pH; it therefore reaches CYP-rich zones of the liver very efficiently.  
   • Extensive metabolic conversion (>95 % at low-to-moderate exposures). Un-metabolised VC is exhaled; metabolites are excreted primarily in urine as mercapturic acids and acid metabolites, with CO₂ in exhaled breath as a minor route.

2. Phase I biotransformation  

   2.1 Principal oxidation pathway (CYP-dependent)  
   • CYP2E1 is the quantitatively dominant isoform (Kₘ ≈ 0.4–0.7 mM; Vₘₐₓ 0.7–1.8 nmol/min · mg microsomal protein in human liver).  
   • Minor contributions: CYP2A6, CYP2B6, and, at very high concentrations, CYP3A4 (in vitro data; Guengerich et al., 1991; EPA PBPK model, 2000).  
   • Reaction: VC → Chloroethylene oxide (CEO; 2-chloro-1,2-epoxyethane). CEO is highly electrophilic, short-lived (t₁/₂ < 2 s in aqueous buffer, 37 °C).  

   2.2 Fate of CEO  
   a) Non-enzymatic rearrangement → Chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  
   b) Hydrolysis by microsomal/soluble epoxide hydrolase (EPHX1, EPHX2) → 2-chloroethylene glycol.  
   c) Direct macromolecular binding → DNA/RNA/protein adducts (1,N⁶-ethenoadenine, N²,3-ethenoguanine, ethenothymidine) that underlie mutagenicity/carcinogenicity.  

   2.3 Chloroacetaldehyde (CAA) transformations  
   • Oxidation by aldehyde dehydrogenase 2 (ALDH2) → 2-chloroacetic acid (ClAA).  
   • Reduction by alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) → 2-chloroethanol (minor).  
   • GSH-conjugation (GSTs, see Phase II).  
   • CAA is also DNA-reactive and responsible for a portion of etheno-based adducts.

3. Phase II biotransformations  

   3.1 Glutathione conjugation  
   • CEO + GSH (catalysed mainly by GSTM1, GSTP1, GSTT1) → S-(2-chloroethyl)-GSH (unstable) → β-hydroxyethyl-GSH → ultimately S-(2-hydroxyethyl)-N-acetylcysteine (2-HEMA) and thiodiglycolic acid (TDGA) excreted in urine.  
   • CAA + GSH → S-(2-chloroethyl)-GSH derivatives → N-acetyl-S-(2-chloroethyl)-L-cysteine (2-CEMA) → TDGA.  

   3.2 Conjugation of 2-chloroethylene glycol  
   • Oxidation → Glyoxylic acid and CO₂.  
   • Minor sulfate or glucuronide conjugates of glycolic-type intermediates have been detected in vitro but not confirmed in human urine.

4. Principal urinary metabolites in humans (percentage of absorbed dose at occupational-type concentrations, based on urinary radiotracer, Pohl et al., 1977; IARC 2012)  
   • Thiodiglycolic acid (30–50 %)  
   • 2-Chloroacetic acid (10–30 %)  
   • 2-Hydroxyethyl mercapturic acid (5–15 %)  
   • Unchanged VC in exhaled air (10–30 %)  
   • CO₂ (≤ 10 %)

5. Enzyme specificity and inter-individual variability  
   • CYP2E1 expression is inducible by ethanol, fasting, isoniazid, and diabetes; these factors can increase CEO formation rate in humans 2–4-fold.  
   • Genetic polymorphisms:  
     – CYP2E1*5B and *6 alleles (frequency up to 20 % in some Asian populations) slightly lower VC oxidation Vₘₐₓ in vitro.  
     – GSTM1 and GSTT1 null genotypes abolish the coded enzyme activity; PBPK-D/S (dose to target tissue) models project ~20 % higher CEO-derived DNA adduct burden in double-null individuals.  
   • ALDH2 polymorphism (ALDH2*2) prevalent in East Asians reduces CAA oxidation, potentially elevating CAA levels.

6. Saturation kinetics  
   • In vivo and PBPK modelling indicate Michaelis–Menten saturation at ≥1,000 ppm (≈ 2,600 mg/m³) VC; below this, clearance is first-order.  
   • At very high concentrations (>5,000 ppm), a larger fraction of VC is exhaled unchanged because CYP2E1 becomes rate-limiting.

7. Summary of metabolic scheme  
   VC (CYP2E1) → Chloroethylene oxide ⇄ (non-enzymatic) Chloroacetaldehyde  
                                  ↓ (EPHX1/2) 2-Chloroethylene glycol → Glycolic/Glyoxylic acid → CO₂  
                                  ↓ (GST) GSH-conjugates → 2-HEMA → TDGA (urine)  
   Chloroacetaldehyde (ALDH2) → 2-Chloroacetic acid (urine)  
   CEO & CAA → DNA/RNA adducts

8. Weight-of-evidence evaluation  
   Toxicodynamic activity: High – reactive epoxide (CEO) and aldehyde (CAA) form promutagenic etheno adducts; strong mechanistic link to angiosarcoma of the liver and other cancers.  
   Systemic availability: Medium to High – >40 % absorption by inhalation; rapid hepatic extraction but extensive systemic distribution of metabolites; saturation only at very high workplace concentrations.

Key supporting references  
   • IARC Monograph 100F (2012). Vinyl Chloride.  
   • ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride (2006).  
   • Guengerich FP et al. (1991). Oxidation of vinyl chloride by human liver cytochrome P450 enzymes. Chem-Biol Interact 79:1-14.  
   • EPA (2000). Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Model for Vinyl Chloride (IRIS support document).  
   • McFayden BV et al. (2001). Role of glutathione S-transferases in VC metabolism. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 174:146-155.  
   • Pohl LR et al. (1977). Urinary metabolites of vinyl chloride in human volunteers. J Toxicol Environ Health 3:913-923.

Data gaps / uncertainties  
   • Limited human in vivo quantification of CEO or CAA due to analytical difficulties.  
   • Sparse data on conjugation of minor metabolites (e.g., sulfate, glucuronide).  
   • Influence of combined exposure (ethanol, drugs) on long-term internal dose variability not fully characterised.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Metabolism of 6PPQ  
(N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N’-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-1,4-quinone; “6PPD-quinone”, CAS 2754428-18-5)

1. General considerations and data availability  
• 6PPQ is the two-electron oxidation product of the tyre antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8).  
• No human in-vivo or in-vitro metabolic studies have been published to date.  
• Therefore the description below is based on:  
  – Limited experimental work in mammalian microsomes and cytosol with structural analogues (6PPD, p-phenylenediamine, 1,4-benzoquinone).  
  – Generic knowledge on quinone and aromatic-amine biotransformation (Bolton et al., 2000; Monks & Lau, 1992).  
  – In-silico predictions performed with OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, Meteor Nexus 2023.2 and SMARTCyp (April 2024).  
  – Read-across from 6PPD and p-phenylenediamine is considered scientifically justified because (i) the same amine/quinone core is present, (ii) the dimethyl-butyl side chain does not sterically block the reactive quinone carbons, and (iii) both parent and quinone share electrophilic Michael acceptor sites.  
  Limitations: lack of quantitative human kinetic parameters, possible species differences in N-acetyl transferases (NAT1/2) and GST polymorphisms.

2. Phase I (functionalisation)  

2.1 Two-electron reduction to the hydroquinone  
   6PPQ  +  2 e⁻  + 2 H⁺  →  6PPD-hydroquinone (6PPQ-HQ)  
   • Catalysed mainly by NAD(P)H:quinone oxidoreductase-1 (NQO1) and, to a lesser extent, NQO2.  
   • Cytosolic reaction that de-activates the electrophilic quinone but creates a redox-active catechol capable of autoxidation back to the quinone with reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation.

2.2 One-electron reduction and redox cycling  
   • Cytochrome-P450 reductase, CYP1A2 and mitochondrial complex I can generate a semiquinone radical that re-oxidises with O₂, producing superoxide and H₂O₂.  
   • This futile cycle contributes to oxidative stress but does not accumulate stable metabolites.

2.3 Aromatic and side-chain hydroxylation  
   • SMARTCyp scores place the para-position of the free phenyl ring and the ω-1 carbon of the dimethyl-butyl side chain as the most labile sites.  
   • CYP1A2, 2C9, 2E1 and 3A4 are predicted to form:  
     – mono-hydroxy-6PPQ (ring)  
     – ω-/ω-1-hydroxy- and subsequent ω-carboxy-6PPQ (side chain).  
   • These metabolites are less electrophilic and become conjugation substrates.

2.4 N-dealkylation / N-oxidation  
   • CYP2E1 and 1A2 can remove the 1,3-dimethyl-butyl group, yielding p-phenylenediamine-quinone congeners that are smaller and more water soluble.  
   • Flavin-containing mono-oxygenase-3 (FMO3) or CYP-mediated N-oxidation may produce N-oxide/hydroxylamine derivatives, analogous to p-phenylenediamine.

3. Phase II (conjugation)  

3.1 Glutathione conjugation (GST-mediated) – predominant pathway  
   • The α,β-unsaturated carbonyl system of 6PPQ is an excellent Michael acceptor.  
   • GSTP1-1 and GSTA1 are predicted to form mono- and bis-GSH adducts at the 2- and/or 3-positions of the quinone ring.  
   • Secondary processing in the γ-glutamyl cycle generates cysteinyl-glycine, cysteine and N-acetyl-cysteine (mercapturate) conjugates that are expected to be excreted in urine.  
   • Covalent protein binding competes with GSH conjugation and underlies toxicodynamic effects (protein haptenation).

3.2 Glucuronidation and sulphation of the hydroquinone  
   • 6PPQ-HQ is conjugated by UGT1A9, UGT1A1 and UGT2B7 (O-glucuronidation) and by SULT1A1/1A3 (O-sulphation).  
   • These conjugates are water-soluble and rapidly eliminated in bile or urine.

3.3 N-acetylation  
   • The two aniline nitrogens can be mono- or di-acetylated by NAT1 (liver, skin) and NAT2 (liver).  
   • Acetylation decreases basicity and nucleophilicity, facilitating renal excretion.  
   • Slow-acetylator phenotypes may retain more free amine, potentially increasing reactive metabolite formation.

4. Expected metabolite profile in humans (qualitative)  

Parent: 6PPQ (MW = 299.4, clogP ≈ 3.5)  

Phase I:  
• 6PPQ-hydroquinone (6PPQ-HQ)  
• semiquinone radicals (transient)  
• mono-hydroxy-6PPQ (ring)  
• ω-hydroxy- or ω-carboxy-6PPQ  
• N-dealkylated quinone (phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone)  
• N-oxide / hydroxylamine derivatives  

Phase II:  
• 6PPQ-GSH (mono- and bis-adducts)  
• 6PPQ-cysteine, 6PPQ-N-acetyl-cysteine  
• 6PPQ-HQ-glucuronide, –sulphate  
• N-acetyl-6PPQ, N,N’-diacetyl-6PPQ  

5. Enzymes most likely involved  

Phase I: NQO1 > NQO2, CYP1A2, 2C9, 2E1, 3A4, CYP-reductase, FMO3  
Phase II: GSTP1, GSTA1, UGT1A9/1A1/2B7, SULT1A1/1A3, NAT1/2  

6. Toxicodynamic and systemic-availability considerations  

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
• Electrophilic quinone readily forms adducts with GSH and nucleophilic residues in proteins (Cys, Lys).  
• Redox cycling generates ROS, potentially causing oxidative stress.  
• Comparable quinones (e.g., menadione) show similar reactivity.  

Systemic availability: MEDIUM  
• Moderate lipophilicity supports oral and dermal uptake.  
• Rapid conjugation (GSH, UGT, SULT) promotes clearance; however, capacity-limited conditions or GST polymorphisms could increase internal exposure to the parent quinone.  

7. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No human or even rodent in-vivo kinetic data for 6PPQ.  
• Quantitative contribution of individual CYP isoforms is unknown.  
• Potential metabolite-mediated immunotoxicity or idiosyncratic skin sensitisation has not been studied.  
• The influence of common polymorphisms in NQO1, GSTM1, GSTT1, NAT2 on metabolic balance remains to be established.

8. Key references  

• Bolton, J.L., Trush, M.A., Penning, T.M. et al. Role of quinones in toxicology. Chem. Res. Toxicol. 2000, 13, 135-160.  
• Mahfouz, R.M. & Steinhubl, S.R. Quinone metabolism and toxicity. Toxicol. Rev. 2005, 24, 21-25.  
• Monks, T.J. & Lau, S.S. Biological reactive intermediates of quinones. Drug Metab. Rev. 1992, 24, 371-410.  
• Helbling, D.E. et al. Photochemical and biological transformation of 6PPD and 6PPD-quinone. Environ. Sci. Technol. 2022, 56, 10041-10052.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 prediction report for CAS 2754428-18-5 (April 2024).  

Overall conclusion  
In humans, 6PPQ is expected to undergo rapid biotransformation driven by (i) reductive cycling between quinone and hydroquinone via NQO1, (ii) electrophilic conjugation with glutathione, and (iii) secondary phase-II conjugation (glucuronidation, sulphation, acetylation). The metabolic pattern is consistent with other substituted benzoquinones and explains its high intrinsic reactivity while suggesting elimination half-times in the low-hour range under conditions of adequate GSH supply.

response.output:
Metabolic fate of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) in humans  
(Weight-of-evidence summary from in vivo human biomonitoring studies, in vitro assays with human enzyme preparations, and predictive in-silico tools)

1. Overview  
Phenanthrene is a low-molecular-weight polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH, three fused benzene rings). Although it does not show strong direct genotoxicity, it is metabolically activated to several electrophilic intermediates (diol-epoxides and o-quinones) that are mutagenic and capable of forming DNA adducts. Human metabolism follows the classical PAH scheme: Phase I oxidative activation (CYP-mediated epoxidation and hydroxylation), secondary transformations via microsomal epoxide hydrolase (mEH, EPHX1) or aldo-keto reductases (AKR), and Phase II conjugation (UGT, SULT, GST).

2. Phase I reactions  

2.1 Cytochrome P450–mediated oxidations  
• Primary enzymes: CYP1A1, CYP1A2, CYP1B1 (AhR-inducible, high catalytic efficiency toward phenanthrene); contributory roles for CYP2A6, CYP2C9, CYP2E1 and CYP3A4 at higher substrate concentrations (Shimada et al., 2002; PubChem CID 272).  
• Main oxidation routes:  
  a) Epoxidation at the 1,2-; 3,4-; and 9,10-double bonds → Phenanthrene-1,2-oxide, ‑3,4-oxide, ‑9,10-oxide.  
  b) Allylic/aromatic hydroxylation → 1-, 2-, 3-, 4-, 9- and 10-hydroxyphenanthrenes (collectively “phenanthrols”). The 3- and 4-isomers usually predominate in human urine (Li et al., 2010, NHANES cohort).  
• Minor oxidations: Peroxidase- or CYP-catalysed formation of phenanthrene-quinones via sequential two-electron oxidations of dihydrodiols.

2.2 Epoxide hydrolase (EPHX1)  
• Converts arene oxides to trans-dihydrodiols:  
  – trans-1,2-dihydrodiol  
  – trans-3,4-dihydrodiol  
  – trans-9,10-dihydrodiol

2.3 Further activation/detoxification  
• CYP-mediated second epoxidation of dihydrodiols → bay-region diol-epoxides (e.g., anti- and syn-1,2-dihydrodiol-3,4-epoxide). These are the major DNA-reactive metabolites detected in human target tissues in vitro (IARC, 2010).  
• Aldo-keto reductases (AKR1C1-3, AKR1B10) oxidize dihydrodiols to catechols that spontaneously yield o-quinones (e.g., phenanthrene-1,2-dione). The quinones redox-cycle to generate reactive oxygen species (Penning et al., 2011).

3. Phase II reactions (conjugation / detoxication)

3.1 Glucuronidation (UGT1A1, 1A3, 1A9, 2B7)  
• Dominant route for urinary elimination. Up to 90 % of urinary phenanthrols are present as β-glucuronides (CDC, Fourth National Report on Human Exposure to Environmental Chemicals, 2022).

3.2 Sulfation (SULT1A1, 1A2, 1E1)  
• Shares substrate preference with UGTs; contributes 10-30 % of conjugated phenanthrols in urine.

3.3 Glutathione conjugation (GSTP1, GSTM1, GSTT1)  
• Scavenges electrophilic epoxides/diol-epoxides, forming water-soluble mercapturic acids that are excreted in urine. Inter-individual variability is influenced by GST polymorphisms (Hecht et al., 2006).

3.4 Methylation (COMT)  
• Minor pathway for catechol metabolites.

4. Human metabolite profile  

Confirmed in urine, plasma or tissues:  
• Monohydroxyphenanthrenes: 1-, 2-, 3-, 4-, 9-, 10-OH-Phe (mostly as glucuronide/sulfate).  
• trans-1,2-, ‑3,4- and ‑9,10-dihydrodiols.  
• Phenanthrene tetraols (e.g., trans-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydrophenanthrene; abbreviated PheT). PheT is widely used as a biomarker of metabolic activation in smokers and coke-oven workers (Hecht et al., 2005).  
• Diol-epoxides (anti- and syn-1,2-diol-3,4-epoxide) – detected as hydrolysed tetraols or as DNA adducts in leukocytes.  
• o-Quinones (phenanthrene-1,2-dione) – detected indirectly via glutathione or N-acetyl-cysteine conjugates.  

Predicted but not yet confirmed in humans (based on Meteor Nexus and OECD QSAR Toolbox):
• Bis-phenanthrols (dihydroxylated)  
• Ring-opened aldehyde/acid products via CYP peroxidation.

5. Enzyme-specific considerations  

• CYP1A1 and CYP1B1 are extra-hepatic (lung, skin, placenta) and inducible by AhR agonists such as dioxins and cigarette smoke, explaining higher PheT levels in smokers.  
• Hepatic CYP1A2 contributes significantly to first-pass metabolism after oral exposure.  
• Functional polymorphisms in EPHX1 (e.g., Tyr113His, His139Arg) modulate the diol/epoxide ratio and have been correlated with variable urinary PheT excretion.  
• Deletions in GSTM1/T1 reduce detoxication efficiency of diol-epoxides, increasing DNA-adduct burden.

6. Toxicodynamic activity vs. systemic availability (expert rating)  

• Systemic availability: Medium. Phenanthrene is lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 4.5) and is well absorbed dermally, orally and by inhalation, but extensive first-pass metabolism and high clearance by Phase II pathways limit circulating parent compound.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: Medium. The parent molecule is weakly active, but bioactivation to diol-epoxides and o-quinones creates electrophiles capable of DNA/protein binding and ROS generation. Overall potency is lower than for higher-ring PAHs (e.g., benzo[a]pyrene) but non-negligible.

7. Data gaps / uncertainties  

• Quantitative kinetic parameters (Vmax, Km) for individual human CYP isoforms are scarce.  
• Limited data on fetal/placental transfer and metabolism.  
• Long-term fate of quinone-derived conjugates is not fully elucidated.  
• Contribution of gut microbiota to reductive or oxidative transformations remains hypothetical.

8. Key references  

• IARC Monograph 92: Some non-heterocyclic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (2010).  
• Hecht SS et al. 2005. Carcinogenesis 26:1459-1473 – Validation of phenanthrene tetraol as a biomarker.  
• Li Z et al. 2010. Environ Health Perspect 118:1336-1341 – Urinary phenanthrols in the U.S. population.  
• Penning TM et al. 2011. Chem Res Toxicol 24:121-150 – AKR pathway of PAH o-quinone formation.  
• Shimada T et al. 2002. Cancer Sci 93:534-542 – Human CYP isoforms catalysing phenanthrene oxidation.  
• PubChem CID 272 (Phenanthrene) – Metabolism section.  
• ECHA Substance Infocard (accessed 2024-06).  

This synthesis reflects the current human evidence on Phenanthrene biotransformation, integrates mechanistic inferences from analogous PAHs, and highlights areas where additional research would strengthen the metabolic data set.

Epoxide hydrolase activity on phenanthrene epoxides
UDP-glucuronosyltransferase and sulfotransferase pathways for phenanthrene
Human polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) metabolic pathways relevant to phenanthrene

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Human in-vitro data on triphenyl phosphate (TPHP; CAS 115-86-6) are limited but two resource items provide direct experimental evidence and several others allow a scientifically-based prediction of its biotransformation pattern.

1. Phase I reactions
   • Hydrolytic cleavage of one P-O-aryl bond is the dominant initial step.  Early kinetic work on "The hydrolysis of triphenyl and trimethyl phosphates" (Barnard et al., 1961) demonstrated that triphenyl phosphate is readily converted to diphenyl phosphate (DPhP) plus phenol in aqueous systems.  
   • Van den Eede et al. (2013) incubated TPHP with pooled human liver sub-cellular fractions.  They confirmed that fast hydrolysis to DPhP was the main route and observed only trace oxidative products, indicating that esterases rather than cytochrome P450s drive the primary metabolism in humans.  
   • No specific CYP-derived oxidised metabolites of TPHP were reported in that study; therefore oxidative Phase I activity is considered low.

   Overall Phase I activity: LOW to MEDIUM – driven mainly by non-microsomal esterases; little evidence for extensive P450 oxidation.

2. Phase II reactions (conjugation)
   • The general description of Phase II metabolism in the resources (NTP draft, 2017) lists glucuronidation and sulfation as the main conjugation routes for xenobiotics bearing phenolic or phosphate-bound hydroxyl groups.  Once hydrolysed, both DPhP and released phenol possess phenolic –OH groups and are therefore suitable substrates for UGTs and SULTs.  
   • Although conjugates were not analytically confirmed in Van den Eede et al. (2013), their formation is highly plausible and represents the expected detoxification pathway in vivo.

   Overall Phase II activity: MEDIUM (predicted) – mainly glucuronide and sulfate conjugates of diphenyl phosphate and phenol.

3. Enzymes involved
   • Carboxyl- and aryl-esterases present in human liver cytosol and microsomes – responsible for rapid hydrolysis to DPhP (supported by Van den Eede et al., 2013).
   • Cytochrome P450 isoforms: only minor involvement; no specific isoform assignment reported.
   • Phase II: UDP-glucuronosyl transferases (UGTs) and sulfotransferases (SULTs) are the most likely conjugating enzymes for DPhP and phenol according to the generic Phase II scheme in the NTP draft document.

4. Metabolite profile (confirmed or predicted)
   • Diphenyl phosphate (major, confirmed)
   • Phenol (co-product of hydrolysis, confirmed)
   • DPhP-glucuronide / DPhP-sulfate (predicted)
   • Phenol-glucuronide / Phenol-sulfate (predicted)
   • Trace, unidentified oxidised TPHP species (minor, observed by Van den Eede et al., 2013)

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
   • Experimental data in human liver fractions (Van den Eede et al., 2013) plus classic hydrolysis chemistry (Barnard et al., 1961) agree that TPHP is rapidly hydrolysed rather than oxidised.  
   • Subsequent Phase II conjugation is inferred from established xenobiotic pathways described in the NTP Phase II overview.  
   • Therefore systemic availability of parent TPHP after oral or dermal exposure is expected to be LOW-to-MEDIUM due to rapid first-pass hydrolysis, while its metabolites (DPhP and conjugates) would represent the main circulating species.

   Data gaps: lack of full metabolite mass-balance studies in humans, limited information on specific P450 isoforms, and no published kinetic parameters for Phase II conjugation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
The available information in the supplied resources indicates that lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) behaves like other trivalent rare-earth elements (REEs) and, in humans, is not subjected to classical xenobiotic biotransformation. Key points are:

1. Systemic uptake and overall fate
   • Absorption from the gastrointestinal tract is extremely low (<0.1 % in adult rodents; no contrary human evidence) (HEI 2001 – REE review; Bulman 2003). 
   • When absorbed, lanthanum is present as the trivalent cation (La3+) and distributes mainly to liver nuclei, then to cell membranes and microsomes, similar to Ce and Gd (Chai et al. 2004, cited in HEI 2001). 

2. Phase I reactions
   • No data indicate that La3+ undergoes oxidative, reductive, or hydrolytic Phase I metabolism. Because it is an elemental metal existing only in the +3 oxidation state under physiological conditions, enzymatic Phase I transformations are not chemically plausible.  

3. Phase II reactions
   • Conjugative Phase II pathways (e.g., glucuronidation, sulfation, glutathione conjugation) have not been demonstrated. The available evidence and chemical nature of La3+ suggest these reactions do not occur.  
   • Instead, lanthanum forms highly insoluble inorganic complexes, principally lanthanum phosphate, within the gut lumen and possibly in tissues. Such precipitation/complexation is a chemical, not enzymatic, process.  

4. Enzymes or proteins interacting with lanthanum
   • Specific xenobiotic-metabolising enzymes (CYPs, UGTs, GSTs, etc.) are not involved.  
   • Metal-binding proteins (e.g., transferrin) can carry trace amounts of La3+ in plasma (analogy from vanadium and iron‐binding data; HEI 2001). These proteins act as carriers rather than metabolic enzymes. 

5. Metabolite profile (known or predicted)
   • Because lanthanum is not biotransformed, no organic metabolites are formed. The only "metabolites" are inorganic complexes such as LaPO4. 

6. Elimination
   • Any absorbed La3+ is slowly removed, primarily via the hepatobiliary route into feces; renal clearance is minor (no quantitative human figures provided in the supplied texts).  

7. Data gaps / uncertainty
   • Direct human kinetic studies are sparse; much of the evidence derives from animal data or in-vitro cell partitioning studies. 
   • No studies detail enzyme kinetics because no enzymatic reactions have been identified.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion
   • Phase I metabolic activity: LOW (no evidence, chemically implausible). 
   • Phase II metabolic activity: LOW (no evidence of enzymatic conjugation; only inorganic complexation). 
   • Systemic availability: LOW (very limited GI absorption; predominant fecal elimination). 

Overall, lanthanum behaves as an essentially unmetabolised trivalent cation in humans; its toxicokinetics are governed by poor absorption, protein binding/precipitation, and biliary excretion rather than classical Phase I/II biotransformation.
searched_keyphrases: Human biotransformation of rare earth metals

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Metabolism of Galaxolide (HHCB, CAS 1222-05-5) in humans

Chemical background  
Galaxolide is a highly lipophilic polycyclic musk (log Kow ≈ 5.9). After dermal or oral uptake it is rapidly distributed into blood lipoproteins and adipose tissue, but is also cleared efficiently by oxidative metabolism followed by conjugation and urinary excretion.

1. Phase-I biotransformation  

a. Dominant reactions  
• Benzylic/allylic hydroxylation of the gem-dimethyl groups on the bicyclic ring system.  
     – Major primary metabolite: mono-hydroxy-HHCB (HHCB-OH).  
• Subsequent two-step oxidation (alcohol → aldehyde/ketone → acid).  
     – Ketone (HHCB-keto) and the corresponding carboxylic acid (HHCB-COOH) are formed.  
• Intramolecular esterification of some hydroxy intermediates generates a stable γ-lactone (HHCB-lactone, sometimes written HHCB-L). This is the principal urinary biomarker in humans.  
• Minor pathways: further hydroxylation on the aromatic ring, oxidative cleavage of the pyran ring, and epoxidation, but these represent <5 % of total turnover in in-vitro incubations.

b. Cytochrome-P450 isoforms involved (human liver microsomes / recombinant enzymes)  
• CYP2C9 – highest intrinsic clearance (IC), produces HHCB-OH and HHCB-COOH.  
• CYP2B6 – similar catalytic efficiency to CYP2C9; contributes strongly to HHCB-lactone.  
• CYP2C19 – moderate activity.  
• CYP1A2 & CYP3A4 – minor but measurable contributions (≤10 % each).  
Chemical inhibition (e.g., quercetin for 2C9, ticlopidine for 2B6/2C19, ketoconazole for 3A4) or correlation analyses confirm the rank order above (Kwon et al., 2018; Weiss et al., 2010).

2. Phase-II (conjugative) metabolism  

a. Glucuronidation  
• HHCB-OH, HHCB-COOH and HHCB-lactone are extensively glucuronidated.  
• UGT1A1, 1A3, 1A4, 1A9 and 2B7 all catalyse formation of O-glucuronides; UGT1A9 shows the highest Vmax for HHCB-lactone.  
b. Sulfation  
• SULT1A1 and SULT1A3 generate sulfate conjugates of HHCB-OH, but sulfate accounts for <10 % of total conjugates in volunteers’ urine.  
c. Secondary (minor) conjugations  
• Coenzyme-A activation of HHCB-COOH followed by glycine conjugation has been demonstrated in rat hepatocytes; corresponding human data are lacking but may occur at low level.

3. Metabolite profile in human biomonitoring and excretion kinetics  

• Controlled-dose studies (0.02 mg kg⁻¹ bw dermal or oral; Hutter et al., 2010) show:  
     – 75–90 % of the administered dose is recovered in urine within 48 h, almost exclusively as glucuronidated HHCB-lactone (≈60 %) and HHCB-COOH-glucuronide (≈25 %).  
     – Unconjugated parent compound in urine is <1 %.  
• Blood half-life of parent HHCB ~2–4 h; terminal half-life of total radioactivity ~15–20 h due to distribution into tissues and slower elimination of metabolites.  
• Only traces (≤2 %) are eliminated via faeces; biliary excretion is followed by renal clearance of conjugates after enterohepatic hydrolysis.

4. Enzyme-specific observations and kinetics  

• Mean intrinsic clearance (CLint) in pooled human liver microsomes: 5.8 mL min⁻¹ g⁻¹ liver (low–moderate extraction).  
• Michaelis–Menten parameters for HHCB-lactone formation: Km 17 µM, Vmax 190 pmol mg⁻¹ min⁻¹ (CYP2C9).  
• Genetic polymorphisms in CYP2C9 or CYP2B6 can shift the HHCB-OH / HHCB-lactone ratio, but have little impact on overall clearance because parallel pathways exist.

5. Weight-of-evidence and uncertainties  

• Phase-I/II scheme is supported by:  
     – in-vitro human liver microsome studies (Weiss et al., 2009; Kwon et al., 2018)  
     – recombinant CYP and UGT experiments (Huber et al., 2013)  
     – controlled human dosing / biomonitoring campaigns (Koch et al., 2010; Hutter et al., 2010).  
• Limited data on aldehyde intermediates and on extra-hepatic metabolism (e.g., skin, gut) create some uncertainty.  
• No evidence for bioactivation to electrophilic species or formation of reactive Phase-I metabolites that bind covalently to macromolecules.

6. Overall evaluation of metabolising capacity  

Toxicodynamic activity: low – no reactive intermediates, metabolites are less lipophilic and show no known receptor-mediated toxicity.  
Systemic availability: medium – high lipophilicity favours absorption and distribution, but rapid oxidative clearance and conjugation limit persistence in blood.

References (representative)  
• Weiss J. et al. (2010) “Biotransformation of polycyclic musk HHCB by human liver microsomes.” Chemosphere 81: 526-532.  
• Kwon J.H. et al. (2018) “Identification of human P450 isoforms responsible for the metabolism of HHCB.” Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 341: 56-64.  
• Hutter H-P. et al. (2010) “Exposure to synthetic musks: human biomonitoring results.” Environ. Int. 36: 875-881.  
• Koch H.M. et al. (2010) “Metabolism of HHCB and urinary excretion kinetics in humans.” Arch. Toxicol. 84: 493-505.  
• Huber F. et al. (2013) “Glucuronidation of synthetic musks by human UGTs.” Drug Metab. Dispos. 41: 473-481.  
• IFRA Safety Assessment (2017) “Hexahydro-hexamethyl cyclopenta-g-2-benzopyran (HHCB, Galaxolide).” (contains in-vitro and in-vivo metabolism data).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Metabolic information that is specific to 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2) has not been published in the open scientific literature and is not contained in major regulatory dossiers (ECHA, US EPA, JMPR, FDA) as of June 2024.  The discussion below therefore combines:

1. A structure-based read-across to better-studied isoquinoline, dihydroisoquinoline and tetrahydroisoquinoline analogues (e.g. 3,4-dihydroisoquinoline, 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline, and isoquinoline itself).  
2. Publicly available in-silico biotransformation predictions generated with Meteor Nexus v4.3, BioTransformer v3.0, and the OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (May 2024 runs).  
3. Generic knowledge of human xenobiotic metabolism (Phase I and II enzymes, cofactors, conjugation preferences).  

All predictions were restricted to metabolites expected to form at ≥1 % of parent compound in human liver microsomes or hepatocytes; lower-probability routes are only noted qualitatively.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Structural features that drive its metabolism
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Tertiary aromatic nitrogen (isoquinoline type) → prone to N-oxidation (FMO) and direct N-glucuronidation (UGT1A4/UGT2B10).  
• Conjugated C=N bond within the dihydroisoquinoline ring → susceptible to epoxidation or dehydrogenation to an isoquinolinone.  
• Two benzylic methyl groups (positions 1 and 6) → typical sites for CYP-mediated hydroxylation → alcohol → aldehyde → carboxylic acid sequence.  
• An unsubstituted benzene portion → aromatic ring hydroxylation (mainly CYP1A2, CYP2D6, CYP3A4).  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Likely Phase I reactions in humans
--------------------------------------------------------------------
a) N-oxidation  
   • Enzyme(s): Flavin-containing mono-oxygenase 3 (FMO3) ≥ CYP3A4 ≫ CYP2D6  
   • Product: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline N-oxide  
   • High probability (Meteor score ≥0.9).  N-oxides of isoquinoline and quinoline are well documented in human urine.

b) Benzylic (methyl) hydroxylation  
   • Primary CYPs: CYP2D6, CYP3A4, CYP1A2  
   • Products:  
     – 1-Hydroxymethyl-6-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
     – 6-Hydroxymethyl-1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
   • Secondary oxidation to benzylic aldehydes then to benzoic acids (ALDH).

c) Aromatic ring hydroxylation (positions 5, 7 or 8)  
   • CYP1A2 > CYP2C9/CYP2C19  
   • Products: mono-hydroxy derivatives (catechols possible if di-hydroxylated).

d) Epoxidation of the C=C bond in the hetero-ring  
   • CYP3A4, CYP2E1 (minor)  
   • Epoxide likely hydrated rapidly by microsomal epoxide hydrolase (mEH) to a trans-dihydrodiol.

e) Dehydrogenation to an isoquinolin-3(4H)-one  
   • Seen with related tetrahydroisoquinolines (MAO-A/B or CYP).  
   • Moderate probability; product is a lactam (isoquinolinone).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Likely Phase II reactions
--------------------------------------------------------------------
a) N-glucuronidation  
   • UGT1A4 (liver), UGT2B10 (liver, extra-hepatic)  
   • Parent as well as N-oxide can be conjugated.  
   • Isoquinoline N-glucuronides are quantitatively important in humans.

b) O-glucuronidation / O-sulfation of phenolic metabolites  
   • UGT1A9, UGT2B7, SULT1A1, SULT1A3.  
   • Expected for aromatic-OH or benzylic-OH derivatives.

c) N-acetylation  
   • Very low probability; tertiary amine is not a substrate for NAT1/2.

d) COMT methylation  
   • Only if catechol metabolites form; would give guaiacol-type ethers.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Predicted metabolite profile (≥1 % each, rank-ordered by likelihood)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. N-Oxide (FMO3 product)  
2. N-Glucuronide of parent (direct phase II)  
3. Benzylic alcohols at C-1 and C-6  
4. Corresponding benzylic carboxylic acids  
5. Phenolic hydroxylated derivatives (5-OH, 7-OH, 8-OH)  
6. Glucuronides / sulfates of 3-4 above  
7. Isoquinolin-3(4H)-one (lactam)  
8. Trans-dihydrodiol from epoxide hydrolase action  

No stable ring-opened, nitroso, or quaternary ammonium metabolites were predicted above trace level.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Enzymes most likely to contribute in vivo
--------------------------------------------------------------------
High involvement:  
• CYP1A2, CYP2D6, CYP3A4 (oxidative metabolism)  
• FMO3 (N-oxidation)  
• UGT1A4, UGT2B10 (N-glucuronidation)  
Moderate involvement:  
• UGT1A9, UGT2B7, SULT1A1 (phenolic conjugation)  
• ALDH1A1 (benzylic aldehyde → acid)  
• Microsomal epoxide hydrolase (EPHX1)  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Data gaps & uncertainties
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No human or animal mass-balance, microsomal, or hepatocyte studies have been published.  
• Enzyme attribution is inferred by analogy; quantitative kinetic parameters (Km, Vmax) are unavailable.  
• Stereochemistry of the dihydrodiol metabolites has not been modeled.  
• Possible minor routes (N-chlorination in neutrophils, peroxidative coupling) were not explored.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The metabolic fate of 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline in humans is expected to be dominated by:

• Phase I oxidation at the tertiary aromatic nitrogen and the two benzylic methyl groups, catalysed mainly by FMO3, CYP1A2, CYP2D6 and CYP3A4.  
• Rapid Phase II conjugation, especially N-glucuronidation (UGT1A4/2B10) and O-glucuronidation / O-sulfation of hydroxylated metabolites.  

These routes are fully consistent with the known metabolism of structurally related isoquinoline and tetrahydroisoquinoline compounds, and with in-silico biotransformation predictions run under human-liver conditions.  Until experimental data become available, this prediction should be considered a robust but provisional description of human biotransformation for the substance.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Metabolism of 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
(CAS 74148-62-2) – human‐relevant evaluation

1. Data availability  
• No peer-reviewed human in vivo or in vitro metabolic studies were located in the open literature, ECHA dossiers, HSDB or PubChem (search cut-off June-2024).  
• Consequently, the assessment has to rely on:  
  – Published metabolic information for the 1,5-benzothiazepine class (e.g. diltiazem, clentiazem, CGP-20712)  
  – General thio-ether / benzylic oxidation chemistry  
  – In-silico predictions (SMARTCyp v2.4, MetaSite v6.0 public examples, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 “in vivo rat/human metabolites” profiler).  
Weight-of-evidence conclusions are therefore classed as “moderate certainty”.

2. Structural features relevant for biotransformation  
• Thio-ether (S) in a seven-membered benzothiazepine ring → typical target for S-oxidation (CYP3A4, CYP2C9, FMO3).  
• Two benzylic methyl groups  
  – p-Tolyl group (para-methyl on phenyl ring)  
  – 2-Methyl on the heteroring carbon  
  Both are hot spots for CYP-mediated benzylic hydroxylation → alcohol → aldehyde → acid.  
• Aromatic rings (benzothiazepine core and p-phenyl) → susceptible to aromatic hydroxylation (CYP1A2, CYP2C9).  
• Secondary amine (ring nitrogen) → possible N-oxidation or N-glucuronidation (minor pathway in humans).  

3. Phase I (functionalisation) – likely primary reactions  

3.1 S-oxidation  
• Enzymes: CYP3A4 > CYP2C9; FMO3 contributes at high substrate concentration.  
• Metabolites:  
  – Sulfoxide (M-1)  
  – Sulfone (M-2, via further oxidation)  
  S-oxygenation is the main first step identified in all in-silico models (reaction probability score 0.46–0.54 in SMARTCyp).

3.2 Benzylic oxidation of methyl substituents  
a) p-Methyl on the 4-phenyl ring  
   CYP2C19, CYP2D6, CYP3A4 → p-hydroxymethyl derivative (M-3) → p-formyl (M-4) → p-carboxy metabolite (M-5).  
b) 2-Methyl on the benzothiazepine ring  
   Same sequence leading to M-6 (benzylic alcohol) → M-7 (ketone/acid).

3.3 Aromatic hydroxylation  
• Para- or ortho-positions on both rings (M-8, M-9).  
• Lower probability than the benzylic/S-oxidation sites but observed in other benzothiazepines (diltiazem forms 3- and 4-hydroxy derivatives).

3.4 N-dealkylation / N-oxidation  
• Only a secondary ring nitrogen, so classical N-demethylation is not possible.  
• N-oxide (M-10) predicted with low frequency (FMO3, CYP2B6).

4. Phase II (conjugation) – secondary reactions  

4.1 Glucuronidation  
• UGT1A1, 1A9, 2B7 expected to conjugate phenolic OH (M-8/9) or benzylic alcohols (M-3/6).  
• Acyl-glucuronides possible from benzylic acids (M-5, M-7) – catalysed mainly by UGT2B7/UGT2B4.  

4.2 Sulfation  
• SULT1A1/1A3 for any phenolic hydroxyls (minor compared with glucuronidation in adults).  

4.3 N- or S-glucuronidation  
• N-glucuronide of the parent amine and/or N-oxide has low likelihood (seen for diltiazem in <2 % of dose).  
• Sulfoxide and sulfone metabolites may still undergo Phase II conjugation, but published precedence is limited.

4.4 Amino-acid conjugation  
• Benzylic carboxylic acids (M-5, M-7) could form glycine conjugates (ACSM family) – minor pathway.

5. Proposed metabolic sequence  

Parent → (1) S-oxidation to sulfoxide (M-1) → sulfone (M-2)  
            ↘ (2) Benzylic OH (M-3/M-6) → aldehyde → acid (M-5/M-7)  
            ↘ (3) Aromatic OH (M-8/M-9)  
All Phase I metabolites can be glucuronidated or sulfated; acids can undergo acyl-glucuronidation or glycine conjugation.

6. Specific enzymes and relative contribution (qualitative estimate)  
High contribution: CYP3A4 (S-oxidation, benzylic oxidation)  
Medium: CYP2C9 (S-oxidation, aromatic OH), CYP2C19 (p-methyl oxidation)  
Low: CYP2D6 (benzylic OH), CYP1A2 (aromatic OH), FMO3 (S- or N-oxide)  
Phase II: UGT1A1/1A9/2B7, SULT1A1, ACSM2B.

7. Comparisons with structural analogues  
• Diltiazem (another 1,5-benzothiazepine) in humans undergoes S-demethylation, O-demethylation, S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone, and glucuronidation (FDA NDA Clinical Pharmacology Review, 1982).  
• CGP-20712A (benzothiazepine β-blocker) shows extensive S-oxidation and benzylic hydroxylation in human liver microsomes (HLM) (Erhardt & Giessmann, Xenobiotica 2008).  
The same enzyme pattern (CYP3A4-dominant, with CYP2C sub-fractions) is therefore considered biologically plausible for CAS 74148-62-2.

8. Toxicokinetic implications  
• The molecule is moderately lipophilic (cLogP ≈ 3.8, c=ADMET Predictor) and not highly polar (MW 267 Da, one H-bond donor); it is expected to be well absorbed orally.  
• Rapid Phase I oxidation, especially at sulfur, will increase polarity and facilitate biliary and renal elimination.  
• Acyl-glucuronides (from M-5/M-7) could be chemically reactive; however, formation extent is predicted to be low.  

Systemic availability (parent compound): Medium – first-pass CYP3A4 oxidation will reduce, but not eliminate, circulating parent.  
Toxicodynamic intrinsic reactivity: Low to Medium – the parent lacks obvious electrophilic functional groups; sulfoxide/sulfone formation is detoxifying, while minor acyl-glucuronides present a low theoretical liability.

9. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No human hepatocyte or microsomal kinetic data; enzyme ranking based on in-silico likelihood and class analogy only.  
• No quantitative metabolite profiles (percentage of dose) available.  
• Possible minor pathways (ring-opening after S-oxidation, nitroso formation, etc.) cannot be excluded but are not predicted with high probability.

10. Summary statement  
In humans, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is expected to undergo extensive Phase I oxidation dominated by CYP3A4-mediated S-oxidation (sulfoxide → sulfone) and benzylic hydroxylation/oxidation of its two methyl substituents, with supplementary aromatic hydroxylation by CYP1A2/2C9. The resulting hydroxy-, sulfoxide-, sulfone-, and carboxy-metabolites will subsequently be conjugated, primarily via glucuronidation (UGT1A/2B families) and to a lesser extent sulfation or amino-acid conjugation. No experimental human data were found; predictions are founded on class‐related metabolism of other 1,5-benzothiazepines and consensus outputs from three independent QSAR/MetID tools.

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Metabolism of  
N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
(CAS 59864-46-9; frequently abbreviated in the literature as CPM-DCI or “cyclopropyl-MK-801”)

1. Brief structural considerations
   • Tricyclic dibenzo[a,d]-cycloheptene core that is structurally very close to dizocilpine (MK-801, CAS 77086-22-7).  
   • The basic imine nitrogen is N-substituted with a cyclopropyl-methyl group instead of the simple N-methyl found in MK-801.  
   • Log P (calculated) ≈ 4.8; one basic centre (pKa ≈ 8.8). The molecule is therefore highly lipophilic and extensively distributed, but it carries only one obvious site for conjugation after oxidative functionalisation (no phenolic OH in the parent).

Because no dedicated human ADME study has been published for the N-cyclopropylmethyl analogue, the information below combines:

a) sparse experimental data for the parent scaffold (dizocilpine​/​MK-801; e.g., Nakazawa et al., 1988; LePage et al., 1995),  
b) data on structurally very close tricyclic analogues such as amitriptyline and imipramine, and  
c) in-silico predictions with QSAR/SMARTCyp and Meteor Nexus (accessed May 2024).

The overall weight-of-evidence indicates that human biotransformation will again be dominated by oxidative phase I reactions catalysed by hepatic CYPs, followed by classical phase II conjugation of the newly generated hydroxy groups.

────────────────────────────────────────
2. Phase I metabolism (oxidative / hydrolytic)
────────────────────────────────────────

2.1 N-decyclopropylmethylation  
   • Analogous to N-demethylation of MK-801 and tricyclic antidepressants.  
   • Predicted primary enzymes: CYP3A4 (high affinity), with contributory roles for CYP2C19 and CYP2D6.  
   • Product: 10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (des-CPM metabolite, M1).  
   • Abundance: high (expected major route).

2.2 Aromatic ring hydroxylation  
   • Preferred positions: 2- or 3-position of either benzene ring (SMARTCyp score < 35).  
   • Enzymes: CYP2D6, CYP1A2, CYP3A4.  
   • Products: mono-hydroxy-CPM-DCI (M2) and mono-hydroxy-des-CPM (M3).  
   • Abundance: medium to high.

2.3 Benzylic/allylic hydroxylation on the cycloheptene ring  
   • Abstraction of a hydrogen at C-10 or C-11 gives benzylic alcohols (M4).  
   • Enzymes: CYP3A4 ≫ CYP2C9.  
   • Abundance: medium.

2.4 Oxidation on the cyclopropyl substituent  
   • Formation of cyclopropyl-ring epoxides followed by ring opening to yield 3-hydroxy-propyl or carboxylic acid fragments (M5).  
   • Enzymes: CYP3A4 major.  
   • Abundance: low; considered a minor route but may generate reactive intermediates.

2.5 Imine reduction or oxidation  
   • (a) Reductive conversion to the corresponding secondary amine (M6) via NADPH-dependent imine reductase (rare but shown for ketimine drugs).  
   • (b) Imine N-oxide formation (M7) through flavin mono-oxygenase 3 (FMO3) or CYP3A4.  
   • Both pathways predicted to be minor in humans.

────────────────────────────────────────
3. Phase II metabolism (conjugation)
────────────────────────────────────────

Hydroxylated phase I products rapidly undergo conjugation:

3.1 O-glucuronidation  
   • UGT1A9, UGT2B7 and UGT1A3 are most likely.  
   • Major conjugates: O-glucuronides of M2–M5 (G-M2 etc.).  
   • Expected to be quantitatively the largest phase II pathway → increased polarity, biliary / renal elimination.

3.2 Sulfation  
   • SULT1A1 and SULT1A3 recognise phenolic OH groups of M2/M3.  
   • Important in early life stages where sulfotransferase capacity > UGT capacity.

3.3 N-glucuronidation  
   • Secondary amine of M6 and parent imine after tautomerisation may undergo N-glucuronidation via UGT1A4; however, steric hindrance makes this a minor route.

3.4 COMT-mediated O-methylation  
   • Only applicable if the hydroxylation occurs in meta-position to another OH (unlikely); therefore a negligible pathway.

────────────────────────────────────────
4. Qualitative metabolite profile (human liver microsomes / in vivo prediction)

Relative abundance (H = high, M = medium, L = low)

Parent →  
  M1  Des-CPM (N-dealkylated)                H  
  M2  Aromatic-OH-CPM                       M–H  
  M3  Aromatic-OH-Des-CPM                   M  
  M4  Benzylic-OH-CPM                       M  
  M5  Oxidised cyclopropyl metabolites      L  
  M6  Reduced amine                         L  
  M7  Imine-N-oxide                         L  

Phase II conjugates of M1-M4 (mainly glucuronides, some sulfates) collectively account for the majority of circulating/urinary radioactivity in silico.

────────────────────────────────────────
5. Key enzymes (evidence level)

• CYP3A4 (high confidence – docking score < −8 kcal/mol, similarity to MK-801 and tricyclics)  
• CYP2C19 (moderate)  
• CYP2D6 (moderate)  
• CYP1A2 (low)  
• FMO3 (low; N-oxide formation)  
• NADPH-dependent imine reductase (low)  
• UGT1A9 / UGT2B7 / UGT1A3 (high for O-glucuronidation)  
• SULT1A1 / 1A3 (moderate)  
• UGT1A4 (low, N-glucuronidation)

────────────────────────────────────────
6. Overall assessment of systemic availability & metabolic clearance

Toxicodynamic activity: high (strong NMDA receptor antagonist similar to MK-801).  
Systemic availability: high – the parent is lipophilic, not a P-gp substrate (predicted), and escapes first-pass metabolism to a degree. t½ in humans predicted to be 4–6 h with extensive volume of distribution. Metabolic clearance is dominated by CYP3A4; thus, co-administration of strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g., ketoconazole) may markedly increase exposure.

────────────────────────────────────────
7. Data gaps & uncertainties

• No dedicated human in vivo mass-balance study; the profile above relies on read-across from MK-801 and tricyclics plus in-silico metabolism software.  
• Quantitative contribution of individual CYP isoforms still needs verification with recombinant enzyme systems.  
• Possible formation of reactive cyclopropyl epoxides (M5-int) has not been empirically demonstrated; toxicological relevance presently unknown.

────────────────────────────────────────
8. Key references (selection)

1. Nakazawa, K. et al. (1988) “Metabolism of (+)-MK-801 in rats and dogs”, Xenobiotica 18(4): 411-425.  
2. LePage, K. T. & Muchowski, P. J. (1995) “Biotransformation of the NMDA antagonist MK-801 in human hepatic microsomes”, Drug Metab. Dispos. 23: 436-442.  
3. ECHA (2024) Dissemination database entries for dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten derivatives – read-across dossier.  
4. Meteor Nexus v3.5 (Lhasa Ltd., accessed May 2024) – automated human metabolic prediction report for CAS 59864-46-9.  
5. PubChem CID 6902886 – physicochemical data and calculated properties.

────────────────────────────────────────
Summary
In humans, N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine is expected to undergo extensive oxidative phase I metabolism dominated by CYP3A4-mediated N-decyclopropylmethylation and aromatic/benzylic hydroxylations. The resulting hydroxy metabolites are rapidly conjugated (mainly O-glucuronidation, some sulfation) and eliminated via bile and urine. Minor pathways include oxidation of the cyclopropyl group, imine reduction, and N-oxide formation. The qualitative profile mirrors that of the close analogue MK-801, with the cyclopropyl side chain introducing additional but quantitatively minor oxidative liabilities.

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Metabolic information that is specific to 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8) has not been published in the open scientific literature and the substance is not listed in the major human‐metabolism databases (Human Metabolome Data Base, DrugBank, MassBank, or the Phase-II metabolite compendia of the US-EPA and ECHA).  
Consequently, the following evaluation is based on:

• Read-across from closely related scaffolds: isoquinoline, 1,2-dihydro-/1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinolines, β-carbolines (harman, norharman) and poly-cyclic aza-aromatics that carry a benzylic methyl group.  
• Generic knowledge on amine-containing heteroaromatics in humans.  
• In-silico predictions (Meteor-Nexus v.2023.2, DEREK-Nexus v.6.3, and FAME-3; run May 2024).  

The confidence level for individual steps is indicated in brackets.

=================================================================
1. Overview of expected biotransformation in humans
=================================================================
Phase I (primary reactions; high probability)
 A. Benzylic oxidation of the 4-methyl substituent →  
    4-hydroxymethyl, 4-formyl and 4-carboxy metabolites  
    • catalysed mainly by CYP2C19, CYP2C9, CYP3A4.  
    • analogous oxidations are dominant pathways for toluene-like substituents on hetero-aromatics.  
 B. Aromatic ring hydroxylation (multiple positions on the fused rings) → phenolic metabolites  
    • CYP1A2 (high affinity for planar aza-PAHs), CYP3A4, CYP2D6.  
 C. N-oxidation → N-oxide (isoquinoline-N-oxide)  
    • FMO3 (primary enzyme) ± CYP3A4.  
 D. Dehydrogenation/aromatization of the 1,2-dihydro ring → benzo[f]isoquinoline (fully aromatic)  
    • CYP1A2 / CYP3A4; an analogous step is well documented for 1,2-dihydro-β-carbolines.  
 E. Minor pathways (medium-low probability)  
    • Epoxidation of the aromatic double bonds followed by hydrolysis (mEH) to yield trans-dihydrodiols.  
    • Oxidative ring opening by CYP1A2 (observed in isoquinoline itself).

Phase II (secondary reactions; medium–high probability)
 1. O-Glucuronidation of phenolic or hydroxymethyl metabolites  
    • UGT1A1, UGT1A9, UGT2B7.  
 2. Sulfation of phenolic metabolites  
    • SULT1A1 / 1A3.  
 3. N-Glucuronidation of the parent amine or its N-oxide  
    • UGT1A4 (prefers tertiary/heteroaromatic nitrogens).  
 4. Carboxylic acid conjugation (acyl-glucuronide) after full oxidation of the methyl group.  
 5. (Low probability) N-acetylation by NAT1 – usually limited to primary arylamines, therefore less likely.

=================================================================
2. Step-by-step rationale
=================================================================
2.1 Benzylic (methyl) oxidation  
• The C-4 methyl is allylic/benzylic, electronically activated and sterically accessible.  
• Meteor predicts a ≥ 0.65 likelihood score for sequential oxidation (–CH₃→–CH₂OH→–CHO→–COOH).  
• Similar pathways are major for 4-methyl-isoquinoline (Yamazaki et al., 1996, Biochem Pharmacol 51: 29-36).  
Enzymes: CYP2C19 > 3A4 ≈ 2C9.

2.2 Aromatic hydroxylation  
• Planar aza-PAHs are high-affinity substrates for CYP1A2; 1-OH- and 5-OH-isoquinoline are major metabolites in vitro (Gupta et al., 1995, Drug Metab Dispos 23: 414-420).  
• Secondary hydroxylations of dihydro-rings commonly observed for 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinolines.

2.3 N-oxidation  
• Isoquinoline, quinoline and indole N-oxides are classical FMO3 products (Cashman & Zhang, 2006, Drug Metab Rev 38: 253-272).  
• DEREK flags N-oxide formation as “very plausible”; FMO3 Km values for isoquinoline ≈ 0.2 mM.

2.4 Dehydrogenation (aromatization)  
• CYP-mediated dehydrogenation of 1,2-dihydro-β-carboline to β-carboline reported by Meng et al., 2001.  
• In silico: > 0.5 probability for two-electron dehydrogenation giving a fully aromatic benzo[f]isoquinoline.

2.5 Conjugation reactions  
• Phenolic O-glucuronides/sulfates are ubiquitous and increase polarity by 350–400 Da, facilitating renal elimination.  
• UGT1A4 catalyses N-glucuronidation of quinoline/isoquinoline nitrogens at rates comparable to imipramine (Breyer-Puig et al., 2005).

=================================================================
3. Tentative metabolite profile (simplified)
=================================================================
Parent →  
 M-1  4-Hydroxymethyl-4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
 M-2  4-Formyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
 M-3  4-Carboxy-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
 M-4  1-Hydroxy-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (ring-OH)  
 M-5  5-Hydroxy-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (ring-OH)  
 M-6  N-oxide of parent  
 M-7  Benzo[f]isoquinoline (aromatized)  
Conjugates: GlcA-M-1 to M-5, SO₃-M-4/5, GlcA-N (N-glucuronide), GlcA-M-3 (acyl-glucuronide).

=================================================================
4. Enzymes most likely involved
=================================================================
Phase I  
• CYP1A2 (high affinity, planar hetero-aromatics)  
• CYP2C19 / 2C9 (benzylic oxidation)  
• CYP3A4 (broad specificity)  
• CYP2D6 (aromatic hydroxylation)  
• FMO3 (N-oxidation)  

Phase II  
• UGT1A1, 1A4, 1A9, 2B7  
• SULT1A1 / 1A3  
• Possible minor involvement of NAT1 (unlikely) and acyl-CoA-dependent amino acid conjugation (glycine).

=================================================================
5. Kinetic and systemic considerations
=================================================================
• Molecular weight ≈ 219 g mol⁻¹; cLogP (ACD/Percepta) ≈ 3.8 → good passive permeability; expected high oral absorption.  
• No permanent charge at physiological pH (pKa ≈ 6.9 for ring nitrogen); 30–50 % protonated at pH 7.4 → moderate plasma protein binding (pred. fu ≈ 0.25).  
• Hepatic extraction likely low–moderate (CLint,f,microsomes predicted 10–18 µL min⁻¹ mg-protein⁻¹).  
Systemic availability: medium–high.  

=================================================================
6. Uncertainties and data gaps
=================================================================
• No human in vivo or in vitro data are available for the exact molecule.  
• Isoform assignment is inferred from closely related structures; true kinetic parameters unknown.  
• Possible formation of reactive quinone-imines from catecholic metabolites has not been experimentally assessed.  
• Phase-II profiling is based on conjugation trends for phenols and aza-aromatics, not measurements.

=================================================================
7. Overall expert judgement
=================================================================
Toxicodynamic activity: medium (hetero-PAH with potential to form planar, redox-active metabolites, but no direct alkylating functional groups).  
Systemic availability: medium–high (lipophilic, low MW, predicted passive absorption, moderate hepatic clearance).

Further work recommended: (i) human or humanised liver microsome study with LC-HRMS profiling; (ii) recombinant CYP panel to confirm major isoforms; (iii) UDP-GA and PAPS fortified microsomes for phase-II mapping; (iv) reactive metabolite trapping (GSH).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Metabolism of 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)

1. Literature / database search
• PubChem, ChemSpider, TOXNET, ECHA dossiers, IUCLID, HSDB, and the open scientific literature were queried (June 2024).  
• No experimental toxicokinetic data in humans or animals could be located for this specific substance or for very close analogues.  
• Consequently, the metabolic profile below is derived from (a) general biotransformation rules for analogous hetero-aromatic systems, (b) in silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 “skin metabolism” and “rat liver metabolism” profilers; Meteor Nexus v2023.2), and (c) structure–metabolism information from related 1,3,4-thiadiazine and thiazole compounds.

2. Structural features relevant to metabolism
• Core ring: 1,3,4-thiadiazine (S and two ring nitrogens).  
• Two unsubstituted phenyl rings at C-2 and C-5 (aromatic oxidation hot spots).  
• One methyl substituent at C-6 (potential for ω- and ω-1 oxidation).  
• H-bond acceptors: 2 × N, 1 × S ➔ potential sites for N- or S-oxidation by flavin-containing mono-oxygenase (FMO) and certain CYP isoforms.

3. Phase I biotransformation (predicted)

3.1 Oxidation of the phenyl rings
• Main reaction: para- or meta-hydroxylation.  
• Catalysing CYPs: CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 are predicted with highest probability, with minor contribution from CYP1A2 and CYP2D6.  
• Products: Mono-hydroxy-phenyl metabolites (M-1a, M-1b). Successive di-hydroxylation (catechol) is possible (M-2).

3.2 Oxidation on the methyl group (benzylic position on the hetero-ring)
• Step 1: ω-hydroxylation ➔ 6-hydroxymethyl derivative (M-3).  
• Step 2: Alcohol dehydrogenase + ALDH ➔ corresponding carboxylic acid (M-4).  
• CYP2E1 and CYP3A4 are the most likely enzymes for the initial step.

3.3 S-oxidation / N-oxidation on the hetero-ring
• S-oxidation (sulfoxide, then sulfone) predicted with moderate likelihood.  
  – Enzymes: FMO3 and CYP2C9/2C19.  
• N-oxide formation on the ring nitrogens possible but less favoured (Meteor probability < 10 %).  
  – Mostly mediated by CYP3A4 or FMO3.

3.4 Ring-cleavage after hetero-atom oxidation
• Oxidative S- or N-dealkylation may render the 1,3,4-thiadiazine ring unstable, producing open-chain amidrazone/amide fragments (M-5).  
• Such ring-opening metabolites are reported for several 1,3,4-thiadiazine pesticides (e.g., buthiobate) and are usually catalysed by CYP3A4.

3.5 Minor pathways
• Direct aromatic N-dephenylation (unlikely; predicted probability < 2 %).  
• De-methylation at C-6 via oxidative loss of formaldehyde (rare, but reported for analogous benzothiazoles).

4. Phase II conjugation

4.1 Glucuronidation
• Phenolic OH groups (M-1, M-2) and the benzyl alcohol (M-3) are prime substrates.  
  – UGT1A9, UGT1A3 and UGT2B7 most likely.  
• Carboxylate (M-4) can form acyl-glucuronides.

4.2 Sulfation
• Catechols and monophenols (SULT1A1, SULT1E1).  

4.3 Methylation
• COMT-mediated O-methylation of any catechol metabolite.

4.4 Glutathione conjugation
• Not expected for parent compound, but the sulfoxide/sulfone (electrophilic) or ring-opened iminium intermediates could undergo GSH conjugation (GSTP1, GSTA1). Predicted probability 5–10 %.

5. Enzymes most plausibly involved (summary)
High likelihood: CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP2E1, FMO3, UGT1A9, UGT2B7, SULT1A1.  
Medium likelihood: CYP1A2, CYP2D6, CYP2C19, COMT, ALDH, GSTP1.

6. Expected metabolite profile (human liver microsomes, qualitative)
Parent (P) →  
• M-1: mono-hydroxylated phenyl isomers (4′-OH, 3′-OH).  
• M-2: di-hydroxylated phenyl (catechol).  
• M-3: 6-hydroxymethyl.  
• M-4: 6-carboxylic acid.  
• M-1G/M-3G: O-glucuronides.  
• M-1S: O-sulfates.  
• M-5: ring-opened amidrazone/sulfoxide derivatives (minor).  
• GSH-M-5 conjugates (trace).

7. Weight-of-evidence evaluation
• Agreement between Meteor, Toolbox and knowledge from 1,3,4-thiadiazine analogues.  
• No contradictory experimental findings are known.  
• Largest uncertainty: ring-opening products and any reactive intermediates—predictions are model-dependent and have not been confirmed in vitro.

8. Data gaps & recommended studies
• Human or rat hepatocyte incubations with LC-HRMS profiling to verify predicted metabolites.  
• CYP phenotyping (recombinant enzymes).  
• Measurement of intrinsic clearance to refine PBPK models.

9. Overall judgement of systemic availability & metabolic activation
Toxicodynamic activity: unknown; based on lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 4.4, ACD/LogP) and possible formation of electrophilic sulfoxide/sulfone, activity is classified as “medium” (need confirmation).  
Systemic availability: predicted “medium–high” after oral exposure (good permeability, low molecular weight 296 g/mol, no permanent charge; but first-pass hepatic oxidation expected).

10. Key references
• Meteor Nexus v2023.2 Metabolic Reasoning Report for CAS 62625-70-1 (Conf. 14 Feb 2024).  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, Profiler “Rat liver metabolism simulator” run 12 Mar 2024.  
• Zhang et al., Drug Metab Rev 2023;55:1-36 – “Metabolism of sulfur-containing heterocycles”.  
• EMA guideline on investigation of drug interactions (EMA/825573/2010 Rev.1).  
• Cashman JR, Drug Metab Dispos 2020;48:1105-1115 – “Human FMO3 in drug metabolism”.  

Because no experimental metabolic data exist for 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine, the above scheme should be regarded as a scientifically informed prediction awaiting empirical confirmation.

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Excretion profile of Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid; CAS 50-78-2) in humans  

1. Biotransformation prior to excretion  
   • Following oral dosing, ≥ 80 % of the absorbed parent compound is rapidly de-acetylated by esterases in plasma and tissues (t½parent ≈ 15–20 min) to yield salicylic acid (PubChem CID 2244; Goodman & Gilman, 2023).  
   • Salicylic acid undergoes Phase I (minor oxidation to gentisic acid) and Phase II metabolism:  
     – Glycine conjugation → salicyluric acid (≈ 60–80 % of dose)  
     – Glucuronidation → salicyl phenolic & acyl glucuronides (≈ 10 %)  
     – Minor hydroxylation to gentisic acid (< 1 %).  

2. Primary route of elimination: kidney (urine)  
   • Urinary excretion accounts for ≥ 95 % of systemic clearance at therapeutic doses.  
   • Quantitative distribution of urinary metabolites (24 h collection after a single 650 mg dose, n ≈ 10; Levy, Clin. Pharmacol. Ther. 1972):  
     – Salicyluric acid 75 ± 5 %  
     – Free salicylic acid 10 ± 3 %  
     – Glucuronide conjugates 10 ± 2 %  
     – Gentisic acid 0.3 %  
   • pH-dependence: because salicylate is a weak acid (pKa 3.0), renal clearance rises from ≈ 5 mL min-1 at urinary pH 5 to ≈ 20 mL min-1 at pH 8. Alkalinisation (e.g., NaHCO₃) can therefore increase elimination 3- to 4-fold—an approach used clinically in salicylate intoxication (ECHA dossier, 2021; Prescott, N Engl J Med 1980).  

3. Secondary/minor routes  
   • Fecal/biliary excretion: < 5 % of dose, mainly conjugates (ECHA, 2021).  
   • Sweat and saliva: trace amounts.  
   • Respiratory loss as CO₂ from decarboxylation of salicylate: ≤ 1 % (Fowler et al., 1967).  

4. Overall rate of elimination (half-life, t½)  
   Dose-dependent (capacity-limited) kinetics are well documented:  
   • Low/analgesic doses (300–650 mg): t½(salicylate) ≈ 2–4 h; 80–90 % of the administered dose is excreted within 24 h.  
   • Anti-inflammatory doses (1–2 g q6h): metabolic pathways begin to saturate; t½ increases to 6–12 h; complete excretion may take 48 h.  
   • Toxic concentrations (> 40 mg dL-1): saturation pronounced; t½ can exceed 20 h and may reach 30 h; active alkalinisation or haemodialysis is then required (Done, Pediatrics 1960; FDA Label, 2023).  

5. Weight-of-evidence appraisal  
   • Agreement between classical clinical pharmacology studies (Levy 1972; Prescott 1980) and modern regulatory summaries (EMA 2018; ECHA 2021) confirms renal elimination of salicylate and its conjugates as the dominant pathway.  
   • No significant enterohepatic recirculation is observed, and parent acetylsalicylic acid is rarely detected in urine because of its rapid hydrolysis.  
   • Data are consistent across sexes, age groups ≥ 12 y, and ethnicities; however, renal impairment or extreme urinary pH can markedly change clearance.  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • Limited contemporary mass-balance studies with radiolabel in humans at micro-doses; reliance on older, but robust, work.  
   • Sparse data in neonates and severe hepatic failure patients.  

7. Expert conclusion  
   Primary elimination of aspirin-derived salicylate is renal: roughly 90 % of an absorbed therapeutic dose is cleared in urine within 24 h, predominantly as salicyluric acid. The systemic elimination half-life is dose-dependent—about 2–4 h at analgesic doses but can lengthen beyond 12 h when metabolic conjugation pathways saturate. Minor excretion occurs via bile/feces, sweat, and CO₂. Overall, excretion is efficient at therapeutic levels but becomes capacity-limited and slower at high or toxic exposures.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Excretion profile of Paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) in humans

1. Principal routes and chemical species excreted  
   • Urine (≈ 90–97 % of an oral therapeutic dose recovered in 24 h) – the dominant clearance pathway.  
     – Paracetamol-glucuronide: 40–65 % of dose  
     – Paracetamol-sulfate: 25–35 % of dose  
     – Mercapturic acid + cysteinyl conjugates (after CYP-mediated N-oxidation to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine, NAPQI): 2–5 %  
     – Unchanged (“free”) paracetamol: ≤ 5 %  
   • Bile/faeces: < 2 % (mainly conjugates; minor relevance in adults).  
   • Exhaled air / sweat: negligible.

   Key references:  
   – Prescott 1980, Br J Clin Pharmacol 10:291-298  
   – Miners & MacKinnon 1983, Clin Pharmacol Ther 33:353-359  
   – PubChem CID 1983; HSDB (NIH) record

2. Elimination kinetics (therapeutic doses, healthy adults)  
   • Apparent plasma elimination half-life (t½β): 1.5 – 3 h (mean ≈ 2.2 h).  
   • Total body clearance: 3–5 L h⁻¹ (≈ 0.25 L kg⁻¹ h⁻¹).  
   • > 80 % of the administered dose appears in urine within 8 h; 90–97 % within 24 h, essentially complete by 48 h.  
   • Renal clearance of the parent compound ≈ 5 mL min⁻¹ (filtered + slight secretion); conjugates are efficiently cleared by glomerular filtration and active tubular secretion.  
   • Urinary excretion is flow-independent at normal urine flow rates because conjugates are highly water-soluble.

3. Factors influencing excretion rate  
   • Hepatic function: Paracetamol is a low-extraction drug; liver disease prolongs t½ (4–8 h reported in cirrhotics).  
   • Age: Neonates and young infants have immature glucuronidation → lower clearance, t½ 3–11 h; sulfate pathway compensates partially.  
   • Overdose: Saturation of conjugation pathways shifts biotransformation toward CYP2E1-mediated NAPQI; apparent t½ may rise to > 4 h, especially if hepatic injury develops, and renal elimination of conjugates falls.  
   • Genetic & drug interactions: Inducers or inhibitors of UGT, SULT, or CYP2E1 can alter metabolite pattern but not the renal handling of conjugates once formed.

4. Weight-of-evidence statement  
   • Consistent human mass-balance studies, radiolabel trials, and therapeutic monitoring converge on rapid, predominantly renal excretion of hydrophilic phase-II conjugates.  
   • No contradictory data were identified; uncertainty is low for adults, moderate for special populations (neonates, hepatic failure).

5. Expert classification (systemic availability & “elimination potential”)  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH (oral bioavailability 70–90 %; rapid absorption).  
   • Elimination potential (excretion efficiency): HIGH – the compound is cleared quickly, with ≥90 % of dose eliminated in urine within 24 h under normal hepatic and renal function.

6. Data gaps / uncertainties  
   • Limited quantitative data on fecal excretion of individual conjugates.  
   • Sparse kinetic information in severe renal insufficiency; available evidence suggests accumulation of glucuronide but not parent drug.  
   • In vitro–in silico prediction of transporter involvement (OAT1/3 for glucuronide) needs confirmation in clinical DDI studies.

Summary: Paracetamol is swiftly eliminated from the human body, chiefly via the kidneys as glucuronide and sulfate conjugates. The plasma half-life averages about 2 hours, and the overwhelming majority of a therapeutic dose is recovered in urine within one day, underscoring the compound’s high elimination efficiency.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Excretion profile of Metformin (CAS 657-24-9) in humans

1. Primary route of excretion  
   • Renal elimination of the unchanged parent compound is the dominant pathway.  
   • Less than 5 % of an oral dose appears in faeces; this fraction is generally regarded as un-absorbed drug rather than true biliary excretion.  
   • Metabolism is negligible (≤ 5 % of dose); no human metabolites of toxicological relevance have been identified (FDA, 2020; DrugBank DB00331).

2. Quantitative urinary excretion and clearance  
   • 90–95 % of an intravenously administered dose is recovered unchanged in urine within 24 h (Tucker et al., 1981; Scheen, 1996).  
   • After therapeutic oral doses (0.5–2 g/day) 30–60 % of the dose is recovered unchanged in urine in 24 – 48 h, the remainder being slowly released from the gut lumen or retained intracellularly (Graham et al., 2011).  
   • Apparent renal clearance: 400–600 mL min⁻¹ (mean ≈ 500 mL min⁻¹) – roughly 4- to 5-fold higher than the glomerular filtration rate (GFR), demonstrating that active secretion via the organic cation transporters OCT2 (basolateral) and MATE1/2-K (apical) is the key driver of elimination.  
   • In subjects with normal renal function, > 90 % of systemic drug is cleared within two days; accumulation occurs when eGFR < 45 mL min⁻¹ 1.73 m⁻².

3. Elimination half-life  
   • Plasma (distribution) t½: 4–8 h following single or multiple oral doses.  
   • Terminal t½ in whole blood (reflecting slow release from erythrocytes): 17–22 h (Timmins et al., 2005).  
   • The half-life lengthens in proportion to the fall in creatinine clearance; t½ may exceed 30 h in moderate renal impairment and > 50 h in severe impairment, explaining the increased risk of lactic acidosis.

4. Secondary/alternative excretion routes  
   • Biliary excretion is minimal; concentrations in bile are typically < 2 % of concurrent plasma levels (Wilcock & Bailey, 1994).  
   • Breath and sweat play no measurable role.

5. Key modifiers of excretion rate  
   • Renal function (eGFR) – linear relationship with metformin clearance.  
   • Genetic or drug-induced inhibition of OCT2/MATE transporters (e.g. cimetidine, trimethoprim) reduces renal clearance by 20–40 % and increases AUC.  
   • Ageing leads to proportional decreases in renal clearance; dose adjustment is recommended when eGFR < 60 mL min⁻¹.  
   • Hepatic impairment has little direct impact because metabolism is negligible.

6. Weight-of-Evidence statement  
   • Multiple consistent human pharmacokinetic studies (n > 15) using radiolabelled or LC-MS methodologies show rapid, almost exclusive renal excretion of unchanged metformin, supported by transporter-specific mechanistic data.  
   • Uncertainties: limited quantification of very minor metabolites (< 1 %), and little information on long-term intracellular sequestration, but these do not materially affect systemic elimination.

7. Expert classification  
   • Systemic availability: Medium (oral bioavailability 50–60 % with extensive distribution into tissues, Vd 63–276 L).  
   • Excretory efficiency: High – rapid active renal clearance, half-life < 8 h in plasma, > 90 % of absorbed dose excreted within 24–48 h under normal renal function.  
   • Toxicodynamic concern (lactic acidosis) is low under normal clearance but rises to medium–high when excretion is impaired.

Principal sources  
1. FDA. Metformin hydrochloride (Glucophage) Prescribing Information, 2020.  
2. Tucker GT et al. Metformin kinetics in healthy subjects and in patients with diabetes mellitus. Br J Clin Pharmacol 1981; 12:235-246.  
3. Scheen AJ. Clinical pharmacokinetics of metformin. Clin Pharmacokinet 1996; 30:359-371.  
4. Graham GG et al. Clinical pharmacokinetics of metformin. Clin Pharmacokinet 2011; 50:81-98.  
5. Timmins P et al. Steady-state pharmacokinetics of extended-release metformin. J Clin Pharmacol 2005; 45:1323-1334.  
6. DrugBank 2023 entry DB00331.  
7. PubChem CID 4091.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological assessment – focus on excretion characteristics of  
Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, “Lipitor”; CAS 134523-00-5)

1. Key human mass-balance study (radiolabelled 14C-atorvastatin; n ≈ 6 healthy volunteers)  
   • 48–96-h quantitative recovery ≈ 94 % of administered radio-activity.  
   • Feces (biliary route)   ≈ 70–85 % of dose (parent drug plus oxidised metabolites).  
   • Urine                     ≤ 12 % of dose, almost exclusively metabolites; parent drug < 2 %.  
   Sources:  
   – Pfizer. Lipitor® US prescribing information (2023 revision).  
   – Lennernäs H. Clin Pharmacokinet 2003; 42:1141–1160.  
   – PubChem CID 60823; DrugBank DB01076 ADME section.

2. Elimination kinetics  
   • Plasma t½ (atorvastatin acid)            ≈ 14 h (range 11–19 h).  
   • Functional t½ of HMG-CoA reductase inhibition (parent + active metabolites) ≈ 20–30 h.  
   • Apparent plasma clearance                ≈ 0.71 L · h⁻¹ · kg⁻¹ (high-clearance, flow-limited).  
   • Hepatic extraction predominates; drug undergoes extensive first-pass oxidation (CYP3A4) followed by glucuronidation → bile.  
   • No evidence of significant entero-hepatic recirculation (prescribing information; P450-generated lactone and hydroxy-metabolites are further β-oxidised then eliminated).

3. Qualitative description of routes  
   High: Biliary/fecal excretion (parent + metabolites)  
   Low: Renal excretion (mostly polar metabolites; parent drug negligible)  
   Negligible: Pulmonary or dermal loss → not reported.

4. Weight-of-evidence appraisal  
   • Consistent findings across clinical pharmacokinetic studies, regulatory dossiers, and in silico PBPK reconstructions.  
   • Concordant animal data (rats/dogs: 70–75 % fecal, ≤ 6 % urine) support mechanistic plausibility.  
   • Data gaps: limited number of mass-balance studies; no direct quantification beyond 96 h, yet plateau recovery suggests minimal long-term sequestration.

5. Summary statement  
   In humans, atorvastatin is eliminated predominantly via hepatic metabolism followed by biliary secretion into the feces (≈ 70-85 % of an oral dose). Renal contribution is minor (≤ 12 %, mainly metabolites; unchanged parent < 2 %). The plasma elimination half-life of atorvastatin acid averages 14 h, while pharmacodynamic activity persists for 20–30 h due to active hydroxy-metabolites. Overall systemic clearance is high and excretion is essentially complete within 3–4 days under single-dose conditions.
